metric_value: Went through a box of computer paper to find the single IBM performance estimate sheet that proved the mainframe + DB2 architecture could not support the required terminal counts and average response time
notes: Medium confidence: personal recollection without contemporaneous document in this file

metric_value: Single IBM performance estimate sheet proved that the proposed mainframe plus DB2 architecture could not support the required terminal counts and average response time
notes: This is the evidentiary pivot of the expert witness testimony

metric_value: Testified as expert witness in a hearing in the Florida Senate Chamber, Tallahassee
notes: Specific venue: Florida Senate Chamber, Tallahassee; c.1990-1991 timeframe

metric_value: Unisys lost the bid despite Kastner's testimony
notes: IBM/EDS reputation ('no one ever got fired for buying IBM') overcame the technical argument

metric_value: IBM and Unisys both failed miserably in the $200M fiasco (Kastner's characterization)
notes: IBM and EDS both failed in implementation; EDS is referenced as entity. Kastner frames it as both vendor failure; his metaphor: tipped over the dominoes

metric_value: Congress eventually passed two pieces of Cohen legislation that stand to this day
notes: Kastner uses the metaphor 'tipped over the dominoes' to describe his role in setting the cascade in motion leading to Clinger-Cohen Act

metric_value: Tipped over the dominoes — Kastner's own metaphor for his role in setting the cascade in motion
notes: Reflective memoir framing written January 2026

metric_value: $104 million contract to Electronic Data Systems (EDS) Federal Corp. in 1989 to design and maintain the FLORIDA System welfare computer
notes: Tampa Bay Times citations [1][2] confirm contract value and year

metric_value: Touted as first of its kind in the world; massive centralized computer system to determine eligibility and issue welfare benefits across entire state
notes: Florida HRS FLORIDA System — acronym for the project name

metric_name: Project scope change to centralized architecture
metric_value: By 1990 project scope changed dramatically to a massive centralized system operated out of Tallahassee, creating the architectural bottleneck
notes: Tampa Bay Times citation [1]; scope change is key precipitating factor

metric_name: System crash: April 1992 food stamp distribution
metric_value: System crashed during the April 1992 food stamp distribution cycle
notes: First major public failure; food stamp distribution stoppage

metric_name: System crash: June 1992 after HRS takeover
metric_value: System crashed again in June 1992 after HRS took over operations from EDS
notes: Second crash occurred when HRS assumed operational responsibility from EDS

metric_value: System response times initially measured in minutes — completely unacceptable for interactive welfare processing
notes: Caseworkers needed sub-second responses to serve clients efficiently; minutes was catastrophic

metric_value: Florida set national records for error rates in issuing food stamps; more than $200 million in food stamp errors including overpayments and underpayments
notes: Tampa Bay Times citation [1]; $200M in food stamp errors alone

metric_value: 200,000 ineligible Medicaid recipients on state rolls; $28 million in improper Medicaid payments
notes: Combined welfare errors (food stamps + Medicaid) due to system failures

metric_value: EDS sued Florida HRS for $46 million in unpaid bills; HRS countersued that EDS had failed to deliver
notes: Litigation ended the EDS contract; state cancelled the $110M EDS contract for non-performance

metric_value: By fall 1994 CPU was operating at 95-100% capacity during business hours vs. industry average of 65%; staff worked nights and Saturdays to reduce load
notes: Tampa Bay Times citation [2]; industry average 65% vs. Florida 95-100%

metric_value: Response times eventually improved to 3-4 seconds after heroic effort and schedule changes — still slow for interactive casework
notes: Tampa Bay Times citation [2]; 3-4 seconds still considered unacceptable for welfare casework

metric_value: Two former HRS officials indicted in 1993
notes: Tampa Bay Times citation [1]: August 19, 1993 'Former HRS officials indicted'

metric_name: Unisys distributed architecture vs IBM centralized
metric_value: Unisys proposed more distributed open architecture using BNA and DCA peer-to-peer systems arguing centralized DB2 processing would create bottleneck effect in state-wide distributed network
notes: Unisys correctly predicted that centralized mainframe would fail for sub-second response times across geographically distributed sites

metric_name: IBM/EDS claim: mainframe + DB2 can handle volume at lower perceived risk
metric_value: IBM/EDS strategy leaned on 'no one ever got fired for buying IBM' reputation; promised mainframe power and DB2 maturity could handle volume
notes: Prediction that DB2 on centralized mainframe could meet response time requirements at state scale

metric_name: IBM/EDS mainframe performance claim: actual outcome
metric_value: FAILED: response times initially in minutes; system crashed during food stamp distribution; CPU at 95-100% capacity; national food stamp error records set
notes: Directly contradicts IBM/EDS vendor claims; Unisys analysis proved correct

metric_name: Kastner 1990 testimony prediction: mainframe DB2 cannot meet response-time requirements
metric_value: IBM performance estimate sheet proved mainframe + DB2 architecture could not support required terminal counts and average response time
source_page: Kastner first-person addition + Perplexity research context
notes: Prediction made in testimony circa 1990-1991; validated by system failures 1992-1994

metric_value: CORRECT: response times in minutes initially; CPU at 95-100%; system crashed during food stamp distribution — exactly the failure mode Kastner predicted
notes: Kastner's architectural analysis proved correct; Unisys vindicated

metric_value: Unisys formed from 1986 merger of Burroughs Corporation and Sperry UNIVAC
notes: Both Burroughs (BNA) and Sperry (DCA) had distributed architectures that were superior to centralized mainframe for this use case

metric_value: IBM posted losses exceeding $8 billion in 1993 as mainframe-centric business model collapsed
notes: IBM undertook massive modernization: bipolar to CMOS, Parallel Sysplex architecture development

metric_value: Combines Information Technology Management Reform Act and Federal Acquisition Reform Act; sponsored by Sen. William Cohen (R-ME) and Rep. William Clinger (R-PA); established CIO requirements, capital planning, performance-based IT management; still in force 2026
notes: Enacted 1996; direct legislative response to 1990s government IT procurement disasters including Florida HRS pattern

metric_value: California DMV spent seven years and $44 million on database modernization starting 1987; abandoned January 1994 after auditors found additional $114M and 3 more years required
notes: Companion 1990s government IT procurement disaster; same architectural mismatch pattern

metric_name: Denver International Airport baggage system failure
metric_value: Denver DIA fully automated baggage system: 16-month opening delay, approximately $600 million cost; opened February 1995 using manual baggage handling instead
notes: Classic companion case; 21-month schedule precluded simulation or testing; no backup system

metric_value: FoxMeyer Drug ($5B pharmaceutical distributor) went bankrupt 1996 after SAP R/3 ERP implementation; SAP designed for manufacturers not wholesalers; system processed only 10,000 orders/night vs 420,000 on mainframe
notes: Companion private-sector case; architectural mismatch between SAP and wholesale distribution

metric_value: FBI Virtual Case File abandoned April 2005 after $170 million spent; 318-page Aerospace Corp report found system incomplete, inadequate, essentially unusable under real-world conditions
notes: Shows same 1990s failure pattern persisted into 2000s; 5 different CIOs in 4 years

metric_value: UK National Health Service National Programme for IT dismantled September 2011; initial budget £6.2 billion; actual costs exceeded £10 billion; only £2.6 billion in benefits identified
notes: Largest civil IT program failure in history; same centralized top-down mandate failure pattern

metric_name: Government procurement's fatal reliance on waterfall development
metric_value: Government procurement relied almost exclusively on waterfall development methods; requirements frozen before development began; catastrophic for software in rapidly changing technology environments
notes: Scheduling by desired outcomes not resource-based estimates guaranteed failure

metric_name: Kastner's analytical framework in expert witness role
metric_value: Empirical benchmarking; architecture-suitability-for-use-case; total cost of ownership (TCO); vendor lock-in risk assessment
notes: Four-part framework that Aberdeen analysts applied to distributed computing vs. mainframe evaluations

metric_name: Network latency multiplication effect of centralized processing
metric_value: Every transaction in centralized architecture incurs: network round-trip to Tallahassee + mainframe processing time + DB2 query processing + return network transmission
notes: This is the technical mechanism Kastner identified that made centralized architecture unsuitable for 1000+ geographically distributed sites

metric_name: Castner scalability red flag for centralized mainframe
metric_value: Whether single mainframe in Tallahassee could scale to handle peak load across 1000+ sites simultaneously was a fundamental red flag in the IBM/EDS proposal
notes: Aberdeen methodology: vendor performance claims require empirical validation through realistic load testing, not laboratory tests

metric_name: No one ever got fired for buying IBM — obsolete advice
metric_value: The 'no one ever got fired for buying IBM' safe-bet narrative was becoming dangerously obsolete advice as IBM architectural strengths increasingly misaligned with emerging requirements
notes: This insight from Kastner's work in the 1990s summarizes the broader mainframe-to-distributed transition

metric_name: Aberdeen Group competitive position vs. Gartner/IDC
metric_value: Aberdeen focused on technical architecture evaluations rather than market positioning; Gartner did Magic Quadrant; IDC did market share statistics; Aberdeen did deep-dive technical assessments
notes: This differentiation positioned Aberdeen for exactly the kind of expert evaluation needed but ignored in government IT procurement

metric_value: Established CIO requirements, capital planning, and performance-based management in federal IT; active 30 years after passage
notes: Kastner's testimony contributed to the cascade of events that led to this legislation; its durability is central to the memoir's historical significance

metric_value: Conflict between traditional mainframe architectures and emerging demands of distributed high-performance systems in early 1990s; state stuck with underperforming system because switching mid-stream is politically and financially devastating
notes: Vendor lock-in dynamic confirmed by Florida's inability to switch after IBM/EDS failure
